message:
fix(render): update compact tier test for layout dimension changes

Update compact_tier_can_hide_node_text_for_tiny_layouts to verify
data-detail-tier attribute and edge label hiding rather than
asserting no text elements, which broke after layout bounds changes.
Also close beads: bd-2nw.3, bd-106, bd-2xl.7, bd-2xl.2, bd-vb9.8,
bd-1c5.1, bd-2nw.4, bd-3ac.1, bd-2nw.10, bd-106.11, bd-106.12.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2nw.2","title":"Extract minimap rendering from mermaid_minimap.rs into fm-render-term","description":"# Minimap Rendering Extraction

Extract the complete minimap functionality from FrankenTUI's mermaid_minimap.rs (1,474 lines) into frankenmermaid's fm-render-term crate.

## Reference Code
- Source: /dp/frankentui/crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_minimap.rs
- Lines: 1,474
- Purpose: Overview visualization of large diagrams with viewport indicator

## Key Features to Extract

### 1. Minimap Computation
- Proportional scaling to fit overview area
- Node density-based detail level selection
- Viewport rectangle calculation
- Pan/zoom coordinate mapping

### 2. Rendering Modes
- Terminal minimap (block characters)
- Simple minimap (ASCII only)
- Colored minimap (highlight regions)

### 3. Viewport Interaction
- Viewport position calculation
- Click-to-navigate coordinate mapping
- Viewport size indicator

### 4. Performance Features
- Lazy rendering (only visible regions)
- Cached minimap with dirty tracking
- Progressive detail rendering

## Implementation Notes

The FrankenTUI minimap uses:
- Downsampling algorithms for node representation
- Edge simplification (straight lines only)
- Color quantization for terminal output

Key adaptations needed:
1. Remove ftui-style dependencies
2. Integrate with fm-layout positioned nodes
3. Support multiple output targets (term, SVG, canvas)
4. Add streaming/progressive rendering

- Unit tests for scaling/viewport math
- Integration with fm-render-term
- Visual regression tests

 {"id":"bd-2nw.3","title":"Complete Sugiyama coordinate assignment with Brandes-Köpf algorithm","description":"# Sugiyama Coordinate Assignment Completion

Implement the complete Brandes-Köpf coordinate assignment algorithm from FrankenTUI's mermaid_layout.rs to achieve pixel-perfect layout parity.

## Reference Code
- Source: /dp/frankentui/crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_layout.rs
- Lines: ~3,000 (coordinate assignment section)
- Algorithm: Brandes-Köpf (2001) with enhancements

## Current State

fm-layout has basic coordinate assignment but lacks:
- Proper block alignment
- Median positioning
- Compact horizontal assignment
- Type 1/Type 2 conflict resolution

## Key Features to Implement

### 1. Vertical Alignment Phase
- Mark type-1 conflicts (where edges cross)
- Mark type-2 conflicts (long edges)
- Determine median alignment
- Handle multi-edge nodes

### 2. Horizontal Compaction Phase
- Block building (aligned node groups)
- Class separation computation
- Minimum width assignment
- Sink-based coordinate shifting

### 3. Four-Way Computation
- Upper-left alignment
- Upper-right alignment
- Lower-left alignment
- Lower-right alignment
- Final: median of four positions

### 4. Edge Straightening
- Long edge dummy alignment
- Port assignment
- Bend minimization

## Algorithm Properties (Alien-Artifact-Coding)

- Time complexity: O(n) where n = nodes + dummy nodes
- Space complexity: O(n)
- Determinism: Stable ordering guarantees identical output
- Proof: Each phase preserves layer assignment invariant

## Implementation Notes

FrankenTUI uses bidirectional sweep with:
1. LEFT_UP, RIGHT_UP, LEFT_DOWN, RIGHT_DOWN passes
2. Median of 4 positions for final coordinates
3. Compact representation via class graph

Critical: Preserve exact FrankenTUI behavior for determinism verification.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All 4-way alignment implemented
- Visual parity with FrankenTUI reference
- Property tests for invariants
- Performance: <10ms for 100-node graphs
- Determinism proof (golden tests)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:15:44.540525239Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T07:03:30.054442680Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T07:03:30.053728257Z","close_reason":"Implemented Brandes-Köpf 4-way coordinate assignment algorithm in fm-layout. Added vertical alignment (4 directions: UL, UR, LL, LR), horizontal compaction with block/class separation, and median combination. 6 new tests (95 total). ### Algorithm Correctness Tests
1. Vertical alignment phase preserves layer assignment
2. Block building creates valid aligned groups
3. Class separation computation is correct
4. Horizontal compaction minimizes width

### Four-Way Computation Tests
1. Upper-left alignment produces valid coordinates
2. Upper-right alignment is mirror of upper-left
3. Lower-left alignment respects reversed layers
4. Lower-right alignment combines both mirrors
5. Final coordinate is median of four results

### Property Tests (proptest)
1. All node coordinates are finite
2. No horizontal overlaps within layers
3. Layer gaps are consistent
4. Total width is minimal for given constraints

### Determinism Tests
1. Same graph = same coordinates (100 runs)
2. Cross-platform determinism (Linux/macOS/WASM)
3. Floating-point consistency

### Golden Tests
Reference outputs for:
- Simple DAG (5-10 nodes)
- Complex DAG with long edges
- DAG with clusters
- Wide flat graph

- Per-phase coordinate dump
- Block structure trace
- Class assignment decisions","created_at":"2026-02-13T04:29:53Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2nw.4","title":"Implement source span tracking through full pipeline","description":"# Source Span Tracking Pipeline

Implement comprehensive source span tracking from input text through IR to rendered output, enabling click-to-source and precise error highlighting.

## Overview

Every element in the pipeline should carry its originating source span:
- Parser: Token spans → AST spans → IR spans
- Layout: IR spans → LayoutNode spans
- Render: LayoutNode spans → Output element spans

## Key Features

### 1. Parser Span Tracking
- Character-level offset tracking
- Line/column computation
- Multi-span nodes (e.g., chained edges)
- Comment and whitespace preservation

### 2. IR Span Preservation
- IrNode.span_primary: Main declaration span
- IrNode.span_all: All mentions in source
- IrEdge.span: Edge declaration span
- IrLabel.span: Label text span

### 3. Layout Span Propagation
- LayoutNode inherits IR spans
- Dummy nodes carry long-edge span
- Cluster spans from subgraph blocks

### 4. Render Span Emission
- SVG: data-source-span attributes
- Terminal: span → position mapping
- WASM: span query API

### 5. Source Map Generation
- V3 Source Map format for web tooling
- Bidirectional lookup (source→render, render→source)
- Incremental source map updates

## Implementation Notes

FrankenTUI tracks spans via:
- Span { start: usize, end: usize, line: u32, col: u32 }
- Multi-span aggregation for complex nodes
- Lazy line/column computation

We need:
1. Consistent span representation across crates
2. Span merging utilities
3. Source map serialization
4. Query APIs for tooling

## Use Cases

1. Error highlighting: Show exact location of parse errors
2. Click-to-source: Click rendered node → jump to source
3. Hover info: Show source context on hover
4. Diff correlation: Map diff regions to source changes

## Acceptance Criteria

- Spans preserved through parse → layout → render
- Source map emission for SVG output
- WASM span query API
- <1% performance overhead
- Integration with fm-cli validate output","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:16:03.139786220Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-15T15:34:41.954515952Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["diagnostics","parser","source-map"],"comments":[{"id":76,"issue_id":"bd-2nw.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Verification contract (mandatory): this bead must ship comprehensive unit tests, integration coverage, and E2E script coverage with detailed structured logging. Required logs include input metadata, algorithm or mode selection, diagnostics, degradation or fallback decisions where applicable, timing metrics, output hashes or sizes, and explicit pass fail reasons. CI artifacts must be sufficient for post-mortem debugging without rerun.","created_at":"2026-02-13T04:28:00Z"},{"id":95,"issue_id":"bd-2nw.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Testing Requirements

1. Span Propagation Tests - verify span covers node/edge/subgraph declarations
2. Multi-span Tracking Tests - verify span_all collects all references

1. Full Pipeline Tests - Parse to Layout to Render with span assertions

- Purpose: Full-featured terminal diagram rendering

## Current State

fm-render-term has basic terminal rendering (~2,000 lines) but lacks:
- Full shape library (all 20+ node shapes)
- Advanced edge rendering (curves, routing)
- Style application (colors, bold, dim)
- Unicode box drawing optimization
- Cluster rendering with borders

## Key Features to Extract

### 1. Shape Rendering (all shapes)
- Rectangle, rounded-rect, circle, ellipse
- Diamond, hexagon, parallelogram, trapezoid
- Stadium, subroutine, cylinder, database
- Double-circle, flag, note, document
- All asymmetric shapes

### 2. Edge Rendering
- Straight edges with arrow heads
- Orthogonal routed edges
- Curved edges (quadratic Bézier approximation)
- Edge labels (inline, above, below)
- Multi-segment long edges

### 3. Text Rendering
- Unicode-aware text wrapping
- Grapheme clustering for width calculation
- Truncation with ellipsis
- Vertical centering in shapes
- Label overflow handling

### 4. Style System
- ANSI color support (16, 256, true color)
- Bold, italic, underline, dim
- Background colors
- Style inheritance (class-based)
- Theme support

### 5. Cluster Rendering
- Cluster borders (box drawing)
- Cluster titles
- Nested cluster support
- Cluster padding and margins

### 6. Performance Features
- Dirty region tracking
- Incremental redraw
- Virtual scrolling for large diagrams

## Implementation Notes

FrankenTUI uses:
- ftui-render trait system for drawing
- Cell-based canvas with dirty tracking
- Pre-computed glyph widths

We need to:
1. Create standalone RenderSurface trait
2. Implement TerminalSurface with crossterm/termion
3. Add mock surface for testing
4. Preserve rendering fidelity exactly

 {"id":"bd-vb9.5","title":"Improve crossing minimization with advanced heuristics","description":"Upgrade the barycenter crossing minimization in the Sugiyama pipeline with more sophisticated heuristics for better edge routing aesthetics:

1. SIFTING: After barycenter, apply sifting heuristic -- try moving each node to every position in its layer, keep the position with fewest crossings. O(n^2) per layer but significant quality improvement.

2. TRANSPOSE: Pairwise swapping of adjacent nodes if it reduces crossings. Iterate until no improvement.

3. GLOBAL SIFTING: Move nodes across layers (re-rank) if it reduces total crossings. Bounded to prevent infinite loops.

4. EDGE BUNDLING: Group edges with similar source/target layers and route them through shared waypoints. Reduces visual clutter for dense graphs.

5. LONG EDGE OPTIMIZATION: For edges spanning multiple ranks, ensure dummy nodes are well-positioned to minimize bends. Current implementation uses median; try also left-right centering.

6. PORT ORDERING: When a node has multiple ports, order them to minimize crossings of attached edges. Currently ports are arbitrary; this matters for class/ER diagrams.

Quality metric: track crossing count before/after each heuristic. Expose in LayoutStats so users can see improvement.

 {"id":"bd-vb9.6","title":"Implement orthogonal edge routing with channel assignment","description":"Implement proper orthogonal (Manhattan-style) edge routing that produces clean, non-overlapping edge paths:

1. CHANNEL ASSIGNMENT: Between each pair of adjacent ranks, assign horizontal routing channels. Edges that need to turn are assigned to channels to avoid overlap.

2. NUDGE ALGORITHM: After initial routing, nudge edge segments to reduce total edge length and improve spacing.

3. CONFLICT RESOLUTION: When edges must cross, minimize crossing angle and ensure crossing point is visually clear (not on a bend).

4. POLYLINE SIMPLIFICATION: Remove unnecessary waypoints from edge paths. If three consecutive waypoints are collinear, remove the middle one.

5. SPLINE OPTION: As alternative to orthogonal, offer cubic Bezier spline routing. Control points computed from waypoint geometry for smooth curves. Configurable per-edge or globally.

6. SELF-LOOP ROUTING: Special handling for edges where source == target. Route as a loop going out the right/bottom and returning to left/top.

7. PARALLEL EDGE ROUTING: When multiple edges connect the same pair of nodes, offset them to be visually distinguishable.

This significantly improves visual quality over the current waypoint-based routing.

 {"id":"bd-vb9.7","title":"Implement automatic layout algorithm selection","description":"Create an intelligent algorithm selector that chooses the best layout algorithm based on diagram characteristics:

1. DiagramType mapping: flowchart/class/state -> Sugiyama, ER -> force-directed, mindmap -> radial-tree, gantt -> gantt-specific, etc.

2. Graph metrics analysis: For ambiguous types, analyze graph structure:
   - High ratio of edges to nodes (>2:1) -> force-directed (too many crossings for Sugiyama)
   - Mostly tree-like (few back edges) -> tree layout
   - Many SCCs -> cycle-aware Sugiyama
   - Very sparse -> force-directed (Sugiyama wastes space)

3. Override: Users can force a specific algorithm via config. Auto-selection is the default.

4. Fallback chain: If selected algorithm fails (e.g., timeout), fall back to simpler algorithm. Sugiyama -> force-directed -> simple grid.

Output: LayoutAlgorithm enum + selection rationale string for debugging.

 {"id":"bd-vphe","title":"Topological Persistence for Stable Layout Features","description":"## Topological Persistence for Stable Layout Features

Apply persistent homology (topological data analysis) to identify and preserve stable topological features of diagram layouts across edits, preventing "layout earthquakes" where small edits cause large visual changes.

## Mathematical Foundation
Persistent homology tracks the birth and death of topological features (connected components, loops, voids) as a filtration parameter varies. For diagram layouts:

**Construction:**
1. Build a filtration on the node positions: start with all nodes as isolated points (ε=0), increase ε, connect nodes when distance < ε.
2. At each ε, compute the Betti numbers: β_0 = connected components, β_1 = loops.
3. Track when features appear (birth) and disappear (death) as ε increases.
4. Features with long persistence (death - birth >> 0) are STABLE topological features of the layout.

**Application to layout stability:**
- Stable features (high persistence) should be preserved across edits. If a single-node edit causes a high-persistence feature to vanish, the layout change was disproportionate — trigger stabilization.
- Stabilization: constrain the new layout to preserve all features with persistence > threshold. This prevents "layout earthquakes" while allowing local adjustments.

**Persistence diagram:** Plot (birth, death) for each feature. Points far from the diagonal are stable features; points near the diagonal are noise. The bottleneck distance between two persistence diagrams measures how much the layout's topological structure changed.

**Wasserstein distance (stability metric):**
W_p(D_1, D_2) = inf_γ (Σ ||x - γ(x)||^p)^{1/p}
where γ ranges over bijections between persistence diagrams D_1, D_2.
If W_∞(D_before, D_after) > threshold → layout change is too disruptive → stabilize.

## Key Papers
- Edelsbrunner, Letscher & Zomorodian, "Topological Persistence and Simplification" (DCG 2002)
- Carlsson, "Topology and Data" (AMS 2009) — survey
- Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner & Harer, "Stability of Persistence Diagrams" (DCG 2007) — stability theorem
- Ghrist, "Barcodes: The Persistent Topology of Data" (AMS 2008) — accessible introduction

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Implement Vietoris-Rips filtration on node positions (or alpha complex for efficiency).
2. Compute persistent homology using a matrix reduction algorithm (standard persistence algorithm).
3. Or use the `gudhi` or `ripser` crate binding (if available for Rust), or implement from scratch for educational value.
4. After each edit: compute persistence diagram of new layout, compare with pre-edit diagram.
5. If Wasserstein distance > threshold: add constraints to preserve high-persistence features, re-run layout.
6. Expose persistence visualization in demo: show barcode diagram alongside layout.
7. Decision contract: adopt if layout stability measurably improved (fewer "earthquake" events) AND computation overhead < 50ms per edit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Persistent homology computed for 2D node positions (β_0 and β_1)
- [ ] Persistence diagram correctly identifies stable clusters and loops in layout
- [ ] Wasserstein distance computed between pre/post edit persistence diagrams
- [ ] Layout stabilization constraint prevents high-persistence feature destruction
- [ ] Computation time < 50ms for 1000-node diagrams
- [ ] Demo visualization: persistence barcode displayed alongside diagram
- [ ] Decision contract: specific adoption threshold for stability improvement","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:33.184400183Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:09.440964849Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","layout","stability","topological-persistence"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-vphe","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:09.440900499Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-vphe","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:52.140354065Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-vvx9","title":"Implement gantt SVG rendering with time axis, dependencies, and today marker","description":"# Gantt SVG Rendering

## What mermaid-js Renders
- Horizontal time axis with date labels (auto-scaled: days/weeks/months)
- Section swimlanes with labels
- Task bars: colored by type (blue=normal, green=done, red=critical, gray=active)
- Milestone: diamond markers on timeline
- Dependency arrows between task bars
- Progress fill (partial bar for % complete)
- Today marker: vertical red/orange dashed line
- Weekend/holiday shading on background
- Grid lines for time divisions

## Current State
- Layout positions tasks as generic nodes
- SVG renders basic rectangles and labels
- No time axis, no date labels, no dependency arrows, no type-based coloring

## Implementation Plan
1. **SVG Renderer**: Draw time axis at top with date labels. Draw section backgrounds. Draw task bars with type-based fill colors. Draw milestone diamonds. Draw dependency arrows (right edge of predecessor → left edge of successor). Draw today marker if date is within range.
2. **Tests**: Verify SVG output contains time axis, colored bars, dependency arrows.
